message:
feat(tui): comprehensive improvements across all TUI screens and chrome

Major overhaul of the TUI operations console with enhancements spanning
every screen and the shared chrome/widget infrastructure:

- Chrome (tui_chrome): Add adaptive tab windowing with density modes
  (ultra-compact/compact/normal), tab icons per screen, scrollable tab
  strip with left/right overflow indicators, and active-tab accent colors
- App (tui_app): Fix clippy lints (struct_excessive_bools, missing_const,
  write! vs format!), make resolve_export_dir an associated function,
  use safe unsigned arithmetic for inspector navigation, pass ChromeAreas
  by reference
- Widgets (tui_widgets): Expand shared widget library with new helpers
- Screens:
  - Analytics: Major expansion (+861 lines) with new chart types and
    data visualization
  - Contacts: Responsive column layout, summary bands, status badges
  - Archive browser: Improved layout and navigation flow
  - Attachments: Enhanced display with additional metadata columns
  - Dashboard: Richer summary bands and sparkline integration
  - Messages: Improved thread rendering and column responsiveness
  - Reservations: Expanded status display and expiration tracking
  - Search: Enhanced result presentation and filtering
  - Threads: Better thread tree visualization
  - Timeline: Refined event rendering
  - Agents/Projects: Minor styling adjustments
- README: Add table of contents, agent conversation example, testimonials
  section, agent configuration guide, and prose tightening throughout
- lib.rs: Module-level adjustments for new screen capabilities
- Compose/hit-regions/persist/poller: Minor supporting changes

23 files changed, +3505/-940 lines.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"br-2bbt.1.4","title":"Add usage-frequency tracking for command palette ranking","description":"DEPENDS ON br-2bbt.1.2 which wires HintRanker into CommandPalette ordering.
This bead adds PERSISTENCE and ANALYTICS on top of the HintRanker integration.

## Task: Persist Usage Data and Add Decay

1. Persist HintRanker state to tui_persist.rs:
   - On TUI shutdown: serialize usage counters to persist file
   - On TUI startup: deserialize and restore HintRanker state
   - Format: HashMap<String, (u32, i64)> — action_id -> (usage_count, last_used_micros)

2. Recency decay:
   - Check if HintRanker handles decay internally (via RankingEvidence)
   - If not: implement decaying weight with half-life of 1 hour
   - Actions used in last hour rank higher than actions used yesterday

3. Usage analytics (optional):
   - Add palette usage stats to ToolMetrics or a new section in Dashboard
   - Show: top 10 most-used palette actions, total palette invocations
   - Useful for operators to understand their own workflow

## Tests
- Unit: persistence round-trip (save usage data, reload, verify counters preserved)
- Unit: recency decay reduces weight of old actions
- Unit: corrupted persist file falls back gracefully (no crash)

 {"id":"br-2bbt.10","title":"[track] Batch Selection and Operations","description":"## Rationale (from webapp parity)

The webapp at GET /mail/{project_slug}/inbox/{agent_name} provides: checkbox bulk selection on messages, dynamic selection UI showing selected count, quick action buttons (Mark Selected as Read, Mark All Read), Select All / Clear buttons.

The TUI currently has no multi-select capability. This is a significant interaction gap for operators managing many messages or reservations.

## Implementation

1. Selection model (reusable across screens):
   - SelectionState<T> { selected: HashSet<T>, select_mode: bool }
   - Space: toggle current item selection
   - v: enter/exit visual selection mode (like vim visual mode)
     In visual mode, arrow keys extend selection range automatically
     This avoids Shift+Arrow which many terminal emulators intercept
     for text selection and DO NOT pass through to the application.
   - Shift+A: select all visible items (NOT Ctrl+A which conflicts with tmux prefix and readline home)
   - Shift+C: clear entire selection
   - Selection count shown in status bar: 5 of 42 selected

   KEYBINDING NOTE: v for visual mode is used on Messages, Reservations, AND Timeline screens.
   On the Timeline screen, V (CAPITAL V) is used for Events/Commits/Combined view toggle
   (br-2bbt.16). Lowercase v is always visual selection. These do NOT conflict because
   case matters.

   TERMINAL COMPATIBILITY NOTE: Shift+Arrow keys are unreliable across terminals.
   Some terminals (iTerm2, Ghostty, WezTerm) pass them through, but many (basic xterm,
   older terminals) use them for text selection. The v-for-visual-mode approach is the
   portable alternative. Shift+A and Shift+C are more reliable because they use
   Shift+Letter which most terminals pass through correctly.

2. Visual indicators:
   - Selected items have checkbox indicator: [x] vs [ ]
   - Selected items have highlighted/inverted background
   - Selection count badge in screen header area
   - When selection is active, status bar shows available batch actions

3. Batch actions per screen (triggered via action menu . key or hotkey):
   Messages:
   - Batch acknowledge (a): acknowledge all selected messages
   - Batch mark read (r): mark all selected as read
   - Batch mark unread (u): mark all selected as unread

   Reservations:
   - Batch release (r): release all selected reservations
   - Batch renew (n): extend TTL on all selected

   Timeline:
   - Batch copy (y): copy selected events text to clipboard (OSC 52)

4. Confirmation modal (from br-2bbt.5):
   - All batch actions show confirmation: Acknowledge 5 messages?
   - Modal shows action name, count, and list of affected item summaries (first 5)
   - On confirm: execute sequentially, show progress toast
   - On partial failure: show error toast with failure count and details

5. Batch action execution:
   - Execute sequentially (not parallel) to avoid DB contention
   - Progress: show toast updating count: Acknowledging 3 of 5...
   - On partial failure: report which items failed, keep selection on failed items
   - Clear selection on full success, show success toast with count

## Tests
- Unit: SelectionState toggle single item
- Unit: SelectionState visual mode extends selection correctly
- Unit: SelectionState select-all and clear-all
- Unit: SelectionState does not include filtered-out items
- Unit: batch acknowledge executes on all selected, returns success/failure counts
- Unit: batch operation rollback keeps selection on failed items
- Unit: v (lowercase) and V (uppercase) are distinct on Timeline screen
- Snapshot: message list with 3 items selected showing checkboxes and count badge
- E2E: select 3 messages via Space, press . for action menu, choose batch acknowledge, confirm modal, verify all 3 acked","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Test and benchmark outputs include detailed structured diagnostics (scenario IDs, timings, error/reason codes, artifact paths, and repro commands)

Plan-space hardening additions:
- [ ] Comprehensive unit tests cover happy-path behavior, edge cases, invariants, and failure modes for this bead scope
- [ ] Integration/E2E (or PTY/stress where applicable) scripts validate end-user flows, degraded-mode behavior, and recovery paths for this bead","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ProudCompass","created_at":"2026-02-12T00:52:36.043559028Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-16T23:05:15.906392747Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["batch","tui","ux"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-2bbt.10","depends_on_id":"br-2bbt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T00:52:36.043559028Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-2bbt.10","depends_on_id":"br-2bbt.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T00:53:54.031338413Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-2bbt.10","depends_on_id":"br-2bbt.5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T00:53:10.871117330Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":762,"issue_id":"br-2bbt.10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Implemented Reservations+Timeline multi-select slice: Space/v/Shift+A/Shift+C selection model wiring, selected-count headers, checkbox row markers, and batch contextual actions in tui_action_menu (renew/release selected reservations, copy selected timeline rows). Added focused unit tests in tui_action_menu.rs, reservations.rs, and timeline.rs for selection transitions and batch action context switching. Validation note: prior targeted tests for these modules were green; current rch cargo check is blocked by unrelated baseline compile drift (ArchiveBrowser / ComposeToAgent exhaustive match + module wiring) outside this bead slice. Keeping in_progress pending full acceptance proof (incl. E2E/diagnostics checklist).","created_at":"2026-02-16T23:05:15Z"}]}

The current TUI has strong markdown rendering tests (26) but no snapshot tests, no widget rendering tests, and no TUI-specific E2E tests. Each new feature needs dedicated test coverage.

## Scope

1. Snapshot tests (new):
   - Capture rendered output of each new widget at standard terminal sizes
   - Compare against golden files
   - Detect visual regressions automatically
   - Use ftui_harness for deterministic rendering

2. Unit tests per feature:
   - CommandRegistry: entry registration, filtering, fuzzy matching
   - Toast pipeline: lifecycle, queue management, severity filtering, event mapping
   - DataProvider: pagination, caching, prefetch, invalidation
   - ModalManager: focus trapping, action dispatch, state machine
   - ActionMenu: per-screen action sets, navigation, execution
   - Animations: state machine transitions, reduced-motion handling

3. E2E tests (tests/e2e/test_tui_v2.sh):
   - Open command palette, search, navigate
   - Trigger event, verify toast appears
   - Scroll through large dataset (virtualization)
   - Open action menu, execute action
   - Force-release with confirmation modal
   - Comprehensive logging (assertion details, timing, screenshots)

4. Performance benchmarks:
   - Frame render time with 10K items (p50/p95/p99)
   - Command palette fuzzy search latency (1K entries)
   - Toast overlay render overhead

## Acceptance Criteria
- >80% code coverage on new TUI modules

 {"id":"br-2bbt.11.1","title":"Snapshot tests for all new widgets (palette, toast, modal, action menu, charts)","description":"Create snapshot test infrastructure and golden files for every new TUI widget:

## Setup
1. Use ftui_harness to create deterministic rendering environment
2. Fixed terminal size per test (80x24 for Compact, 160x48 for Large)
3. Golden file format: .expected.txt (rendered buffer as text)
4. Comparison: exact character match with diff on failure

## Snapshot Tests Required

### Command Palette (br-2bbt.1)
- palette_empty_large.expected.txt: empty query, all entries visible
- palette_filtered_large.expected.txt: query "msg", filtered results
- palette_compact.expected.txt: palette in 80x24 terminal
- palette_with_entity_results.expected.txt: agent/thread entries shown

### Toast Notifications (br-2bbt.2)
- toast_single_info.expected.txt: one info toast, top-right
- toast_triple_mixed.expected.txt: 3 stacked (info, warning, error)
- toast_compact.expected.txt: toast in 80x24 terminal
- toast_with_action.expected.txt: toast with action button

### Modal Dialogs (br-2bbt.5)
- modal_confirmation_large.expected.txt: centered confirm dialog
- modal_two_actions.expected.txt: confirm + cancel buttons
- modal_compact.expected.txt: modal in 80x24 terminal

### Action Menu (br-2bbt.5)
- action_menu_messages.expected.txt: message actions list
- action_menu_reservations.expected.txt: reservation actions list

### Native Charts (br-2bbt.4)
- sparkline_20points.expected.txt: sparkline with 20 data points
- progress_bar_80pct.expected.txt: gauge at 80%
- progress_bar_15pct.expected.txt: gauge at 15% (red threshold)
- percentile_ribbon.expected.txt: p50/p95/p99 chart

## Test Pattern
Each test follows:
1. Create widget with fixed test data
2. Render into ftui_harness buffer at fixed size
3. Compare with golden file
4. On mismatch: print diff, write actual to .actual.txt for review
5. Test name includes terminal size for clarity

## Acceptance Criteria
- Minimum 15 snapshot tests covering all new widgets
- Golden files checked into repo under tests/tui_snapshots/
- cargo test -p mcp-agent-mail-server runs all snapshot tests
- Clear diff output on snapshot mismatch
- Regeneration command documented (e.g., UPDATE_SNAPSHOTS=1 cargo test)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:11:33.085028198Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T09:50:54.700815779Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T09:50:54.700788708Z","close_reason":"Implemented snapshot tests for TUI V2 widgets using full-app rendering approach.  {"id":"br-2bbt.11.2","title":"E2E test script: tests/e2e/test_tui_v2.sh with comprehensive logging","description":"Create comprehensive E2E test script for TUI V2 features:

## Script: tests/e2e/test_tui_v2.sh

Uses e2e_lib.sh harness for assertion macros and logging.

### Test Cases (minimum 30 assertions)

1. test_command_palette_entity_search (4 assertions):
   - Start server, register agent, send message
   - Verify palette shows agent in entity results (via MCP tool call + state inspection)
   - Verify palette shows message subject in entity results
   - Verify palette shows thread in entity results
   - Assert palette entry count > static count (proves dynamic entries loaded)

2. test_toast_on_message (4 assertions):
   - Register 2 agents, send message from A to B
   - Assert toast notification created (inspect notification queue state via MCP)
   - Assert toast contains sender name and subject
   - Assert toast severity is Info
   - Wait 6 seconds, assert toast auto-dismissed

3. test_toast_on_tool_error (3 assertions):
   - Trigger a tool call that produces an error (e.g., send_message with invalid project)
   - Assert error toast created with tool name
   - Assert toast severity is Error

4. test_reservation_expiry_warning (3 assertions):
   - Create reservation with short TTL (10s)
   - Wait until within 5 min of expiry
   - Assert warning toast generated for expiring reservation

5. test_virtualized_timeline (4 assertions):
   - Send 500 rapid messages (generates 1000+ events: MessageSent + MessageReceived)
   - Verify event ring buffer has >1000 events
   - Verify server is still responsive (health_check succeeds)
   - Verify no frame render errors in server log

6. test_modal_confirmation (4 assertions):
   - Create file reservation
   - Trigger force_release_file_reservation via MCP
   - Verify reservation was released
   - Verify server processed the action (no modal in headless mode, but confirm the
     modal infrastructure compiles and initializes correctly via unit tests)

7. test_action_menu_actions (4 assertions):
   - Send message, verify it appears in fetch_inbox
   - Verify per-screen action sets are defined (test via internal API/module test)
   - Verify action menu has correct actions for Messages screen
   - Verify action menu has correct actions for Reservations screen

8. test_global_inbox (3 assertions):
   - Create 2 projects with messages in each
   - Fetch inbox globally (if Global mode is API-accessible)
   - Assert messages from both projects returned
   - Assert project context included in results

9. test_search_field_scope (3 assertions):
   - Create messages with distinct subject and body content
   - Search with default field scope, verify results
   - Search with subject-only field scope, verify body-only matches excluded

10. test_notification_queue_config (3 assertions):
    - Set AM_TUI_TOAST_SEVERITY=error
    - Generate Info-level event (message received)
    - Assert Info toast was NOT created (filtered by severity threshold)

### Testing Mechanism

 {"id":"br-2bbt.11.3","title":"Unit tests for all new TUI modules with coverage tracking","description":"Create unit tests for every new/enhanced module in the TUI V2 upgrade:

## IMPORTANT: File Location Guidance

tui_keymap.rs, tui_layout.rs (76KB), tui_macro.rs, tui_markdown.rs, tui_persist.rs (36KB),

## Test Coverage Targets Per Feature

### Command Palette Enhancement

- All 14 MailScreenId variants have palette navigation entries
Target: 8+ tests

### Toast Notification Enhancement
- Event-to-toast mapping for each ACTUAL event variant:
  - MessageReceived -> Info toast with sender + subject
  - ToolCallEnd (slow) -> Warning toast with tool name + duration
  - ToolCallEnd (error in preview) -> Error toast
  - AgentRegistered -> Info toast
  - ReservationGranted (exclusive) -> Info toast
  - ServerShutdown -> Error toast
- Events that produce NO toast: ToolCallStart, MessageSent, HttpRequest, HealthPulse
- Reservation expiry tracking: insert on grant, remove on release, warn at 5 min
- Severity threshold filtering (configurable via env var)
- Ctrl+T focus/unfocus state transitions
Target: 14+ tests

### Virtualization
- DataProvider.total_count() matches source
- DataProvider.window() returns correct slice
- DataProvider.window() out of bounds returns empty
- TimelineDataProvider wraps ring buffer correctly (non-blocking access)
- MessageDataProvider page caching (load, hit, evict)
- Prefetch trigger at edge proximity
- Invalidation clears cache
- RenderItem implementations produce expected output
Target: 10+ tests

### Modal Dialogs
- ModalManager show/close lifecycle
- Focus trapping: key events not passed to parent
- Enter executes selected action, Esc cancels
- Tab cycles action selection
Target: 6+ tests

### Action Menus
- Per-screen action sets: Messages, Reservations, Agents, Threads, Timeline, Contacts
- Navigation: Down/Up moves selection, wraps around
- First-letter jump
- Execute returns selected action
Target: 6+ tests

-{"id":"br-2tnl.5.10","title":"Two-Tier Search Hardening: Thread-Safe Init, Embedder Fallbacks, Progressive Refinement","description":"## Background

Code review of the two-tier search implementation identified three areas for hardening:

1. **Lazy initialization race condition** - `try_two_tier_search` may create duplicate `TwoTierBridge` instances when called concurrently before initialization completes.

2. **Zero-filled quality embeddings** - When quality embedder is unavailable, documents get zero-filled quality embeddings which provide no meaningful similarity signal.

3. **Progressive refinement not implemented** - The current `TwoTierBridge::search()` only uses fast embeddings; the full fast→quality progressive refinement workflow is stubbed.

## Goals

- Ensure thread-safe single initialization of embedder bridges
- Provide meaningful fallback behavior when quality embedder is unavailable
- Implement full progressive search with streaming/async quality refinement
- Maintain sub-millisecond fast-path latency
- Add comprehensive observability for debugging and monitoring

## Architecture Context

The two-tier system uses:
- **Fast tier**: potion-128M (Model2Vec, ~0ms inference, 256 dims)
- **Quality tier**: MiniLM-L6-v2 (FastEmbed/ONNX, ~128ms, 384 dims)

Both embedders use global `OnceLock` singletons for auto-initialization.

- Zero duplicate bridge creation under concurrent access

 {"id":"br-2tnl.5.10.1","title":"T5.10.1: Eliminate lazy initialization race in TwoTierBridge","description":"## Problem Statement

The current `try_two_tier_search` function (search_service.rs:776-793) has a race condition:

```rust
fn try_two_tier_search(query: &SearchQuery, limit: usize) -> Option<Vec<SearchResult>> {
    let bridge = get_two_tier_bridge().or_else(|| {
        let _ = init_two_tier_bridge();  // ← Race: multiple threads may call this
        get_two_tier_bridge()
    });
    // ...
}
```

When two threads call `try_two_tier_search` before initialization:
1. Both threads see `get_two_tier_bridge()` return `None`
2. Both threads call `init_two_tier_bridge()`
3. Both create a `TwoTierBridge` (expensive: loads embedders, allocates index)
4. `OnceLock::set` silently drops the second bridge
5. Duplicate "Two-tier semantic bridge initialized" log messages appear

## Impact

- **Resource waste**: Creating TwoTierBridge is expensive (embedder initialization)
- **Confusing logs**: Duplicate initialization messages
- **Theoretical correctness**: Both threads still get a valid bridge, but wasteful

## Solution: OnceLock::get_or_init Pattern

Replace the two-step "check then init" pattern with atomic `get_or_init`:

```rust
static TWO_TIER_BRIDGE: OnceLock<Arc<TwoTierBridge>> = OnceLock::new();

fn get_or_init_two_tier_bridge() -> &'static Arc<TwoTierBridge> {
    TWO_TIER_BRIDGE.get_or_init(|| {
        Arc::new(TwoTierBridge::new())
    })
}
```

However, `TwoTierBridge::new()` may fail if embedders are unavailable. Need to handle:
- `OnceLock<Option<Arc<TwoTierBridge>>>` for fallible init
- Or separate `OnceLock` for availability check

## Implementation Plan

1. Change `TWO_TIER_BRIDGE` to use `get_or_init` pattern
2. Handle fallible initialization gracefully
3. Remove `init_two_tier_bridge()` public API (no longer needed)
4. Update `try_two_tier_search` to use atomic getter
5. Add debug logging for initialization timing

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Only one TwoTierBridge created under concurrent access
- [ ] Single "Two-tier semantic bridge initialized" log message
- [ ] No change to search behavior or latency
- [ ] Existing tests pass

## Test Plan

1. Unit test: concurrent access to `get_or_init_two_tier_bridge` from 10 threads
2. Verify only one initialization log message
3. Verify all threads get same Arc pointer (pointer equality)

## Files to Modify

- `crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/search_service.rs`
  - `TWO_TIER_BRIDGE` static
  - `init_two_tier_bridge()` → deprecate/remove
  - `get_two_tier_bridge()` → use `get_or_init`
  - `try_two_tier_search()` → simplify","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T22:38:46.402926417Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T01:51:39.726160272Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T01:51:39.726140074Z","close_reason":"Implemented and verified in search_service.rs (atomic OnceLock get_or_init + concurrency tests)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["concurrency","init","two-tier"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-2tnl.5.10.1","depends_on_id":"br-2tnl.5.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T22:38:46.402926417Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":490,"issue_id":"br-2tnl.5.10.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: The OnceLock pattern change should be backwards-compatible. Existing code calling init_two_tier_bridge() can be deprecated with #[deprecated] attribute, pointing to get_or_init_two_tier_bridge(). The test for this should verify Arc pointer equality across threads, not just functional correctness.","created_at":"2026-02-12T22:43:22Z"},{"id":508,"issue_id":"br-2tnl.5.10.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"IMPLEMENTATION COMPLETE (CopperTower): Fixed race condition using OnceLock::get_or_init pattern. Added get_or_init_two_tier_bridge() for atomic TwoTierBridge init. All tests pass.","created_at":"2026-02-13T00:54:40Z"},{"id":516,"issue_id":"br-2tnl.5.10.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Fresh-eyes audit follow-up: get_or_init_two_tier_bridge_is_thread_safe test now asserts full completion of all 10 spawned threads before checking Option/pointer invariants, preventing partial-result false positives.","created_at":"2026-02-13T01:14:22Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-2tnl.5.10.10","title":"T5.10.10: Benchmark suite for two-tier search performance","description":"## Objective

Comprehensive benchmark suite to validate two-tier search performance targets and prevent regressions.

## Background: Performance Requirements

From the bake-off and production requirements:
- **Fast embedding**: <1ms for any query
- **Fast search (1K docs)**: <1ms
- **Fast search (10K docs)**: <5ms
- **Fast search (100K docs)**: <50ms
- **Quality refinement**: <200ms additional
- **Memory**: <1KB per document (fast + quality embeddings)

## Benchmark Categories

### 1. Micro-benchmarks (per-operation)

\`\`\`rust
#[bench]
fn bench_dot_product_f16_256dim(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let embedding: Vec<f16> = (0..256).map(|i| f16::from_f32(i as f32 * 0.01)).collect();
    let query: Vec<f32> = (0..256).map(|i| i as f32 * 0.01).collect();
    
    b.iter(|| {
        black_box(dot_product_f16_simd(black_box(&embedding), black_box(&query)))
    });
}

#[bench]
fn bench_l2_normalize_256dim(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let mut vec: Vec<f32> = (0..256).map(|i| i as f32 * 0.01).collect();
    b.iter(|| {
        l2_normalize(black_box(&mut vec.clone()));
    });
}

#[bench]
fn bench_f16_to_f32_conversion_256(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let embedding: Vec<f16> = (0..256).map(|i| f16::from_f32(i as f32 * 0.01)).collect();
    b.iter(|| {
        let _: Vec<f32> = black_box(&embedding).iter().map(|&x| f32::from(x)).collect();
    });
}
\`\`\`

### 2. Index benchmarks (search operations)

\`\`\`rust
#[bench]
fn bench_search_fast_100_docs(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let index = build_test_index(100);
    let query = make_random_query(256);
    b.iter(|| black_box(index.search_fast(black_box(&query), 10)));
}

#[bench]
fn bench_search_fast_1000_docs(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let index = build_test_index(1000);
    let query = make_random_query(256);
    b.iter(|| black_box(index.search_fast(black_box(&query), 10)));
}

#[bench]
fn bench_search_fast_10000_docs(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let index = build_test_index(10000);
    let query = make_random_query(256);
    b.iter(|| black_box(index.search_fast(black_box(&query), 10)));
}

#[bench]
fn bench_search_progressive_1000_docs(b: &mut Bencher) {
    let index = build_test_index(1000);
    let searcher = create_test_searcher(&index);
    let query = "test query for progressive search";

fn test_memory_per_document() {
    let config = TwoTierConfig::default();
    let mut index = TwoTierIndex::new(&config);

    // Add 1000 documents

    let after = get_memory_usage();

 {"id":"br-2tnl.5.10.2","title":"T5.10.2: Implement meaningful fallback for missing quality embeddings","description":"## Problem Statement

When the quality embedder is unavailable (e.g., MiniLM not downloaded), documents get zero-filled quality embeddings (search_service.rs:717-721):

```rust
let quality_embedding_f16 = quality_embedding
    .unwrap_or_else(|| vec![0.0; self.config.quality_dimension])  // ← All zeros!
    .into_iter()
    .map(f16::from_f32)
    .collect::<Vec<_>>();
```

**Problem**: A zero vector has no meaningful similarity with any query:
- `dot_product([0,0,...,0], query) = 0` regardless of query
- During quality refinement, all zero-embedded docs get score 0
- Score blending becomes meaningless: `0.3 * fast + 0.7 * 0 = 0.3 * fast`

## Impact

- **Degraded refinement**: Quality tier contributes nothing for zero-embedded docs
- **Ranking distortion**: Fast-only scores dominate even when quality should matter
- **Silent failure**: No indication that quality embeddings are missing

## Solution Options

### Option A: Marker-Based Exclusion (Recommended)

Store a "has_quality_embedding" flag per document:

```rust
pub struct TwoTierEntry {
    pub doc_id: u64,
    pub doc_kind: DocKind,
    pub project_id: Option<i64>,
    pub fast_embedding: Vec<f16>,
    pub quality_embedding: Vec<f16>,
    pub has_quality: bool,  // ← New field
}
```

During refinement, skip quality scoring for docs without quality embeddings:
- Fast score only for these docs
- Or exclude from quality-dependent ranking entirely

### Option B: Replicate Fast Embedding as Quality Fallback

When quality is unavailable, project fast embedding into quality space:
- Simple: pad/truncate fast to quality dimension (lossy but non-zero)
- Better: learn a projection matrix (requires training data)

### Option C: Hash-Based Pseudo-Embedding

Use deterministic hash of text to generate a pseudo-embedding:
- `hashembed(text) → [f32; quality_dim]`
- Provides some clustering by text similarity
- Already implemented in `HashEmbedder`

## Recommended Approach: Option A + Graceful Degradation

1. Add `has_quality: bool` to `TwoTierEntry`
2. Store `true` only when real quality embedding is computed
3. During refinement:
   - Docs with `has_quality=true`: use blended score
   - Docs with `has_quality=false`: use fast score only (weight=0 for quality)
4. Add observability: track ratio of quality-available docs

## Implementation Plan

1. **Modify TwoTierEntry**: Add `has_quality` field
2. **Modify TwoTierIndex**: Store `has_quality` in parallel vector
3. **Modify add_document**: Set `has_quality` based on actual embedding success
4. **Modify refinement logic**: Skip quality scoring for non-quality docs
5. **Add metrics**: Track quality embedding coverage percentage
6. **Add tests**: Verify graceful degradation behavior

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Documents without quality embeddings participate in fast search
- [ ] Quality refinement correctly excludes/handles non-quality docs
- [ ] Metrics show quality embedding coverage ratio
- [ ] No zero-vector quality embeddings stored
- [ ] Existing tests pass

## Test Plan

1. Unit test: Add docs with and without quality embeddings
2. Unit test: Search returns correct results mixing both types
3. Unit test: Refinement doesn't crash on non-quality docs
4. Integration test: Verify quality coverage metric

1. DETECTION: Add utility function to scan index for zero-vector quality embeddings:
```rust
impl TwoTierIndex {
    pub fn detect_zero_quality_docs(&self) -> Vec<u64> {
        self.entries.iter()
            .filter(|e| e.has_quality && is_zero_vector(&e.quality_embedding))
            .map(|e| e.doc_id)
            .collect()
    }
}
```

2. BACKFILL OPTION A (recommended): Mark existing zero-vectors as has_quality=false:
```rust
pub fn migrate_zero_quality_to_no_quality(&mut self) -> usize {
    let mut count = 0;
    for entry in &mut self.entries {
        if entry.has_quality && is_zero_vector(&entry.quality_embedding) {
            entry.has_quality = false;
            count += 1;
        }
    }
    tracing::info!(migrated = count, "Migrated zero-quality docs to has_quality=false");
    count
}
```

3. BACKFILL OPTION B (if quality embedder now available): Re-embed with quality:
- This requires storing original text or re-fetching from DB
- More expensive but produces real quality embeddings
- Implement as optional background job

 ## Why This Exists
 
-Modern projects often run multiple coding agents at once -- backend, frontend, scripts, infra. Without a shared coordination fabric, agents overwrite each other's edits, miss critical context from parallel workstreams, and require humans to relay messages across tools and teams.
+Modern projects often run multiple coding agents at once (backend, frontend, scripts, infra). Without a shared coordination fabric, agents overwrite each other's edits, miss critical context from parallel workstreams, and require humans to relay messages across tools and teams.
 
 Agent Mail was the first open-source agent coordination system that works across providers (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, etc.), available since October 2025. You're going to hear about a lot of agent communication systems because it's such an obviously good idea, but Agent Mail sidesteps all the footguns that a naive implementation falls prey to. Combined with [Beads](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust) and [bv](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer), it unlocks truly insane productivity gains.
 
 ### The Footguns Agent Mail Avoids
 
-**No "broadcast to all" mode.** Agents are lazy and will default to broadcasting everything to everyone if you let them. That's like your email system at work defaulting to reply-all every time -- it spams every agent with mostly irrelevant information and burns precious context.
+**No "broadcast to all" mode.** Agents are lazy and will default to broadcasting everything to everyone if you let them. That's like your email system at work defaulting to reply-all every time; it spams every agent with mostly irrelevant information and burns precious context.
 
 **Obsessively refined API ergonomics.** Bad MCP documentation and poor agent ergonomics are the silent killers. It takes a huge amount of careful iteration, observation, and refinement to get the balance so tools just work reliably without wasting tokens. Agent Mail's 34 tool definitions have been through hundreds of rounds of real-world tuning.
 
 **No git worktrees.** Worktrees demolish development velocity and create debt you need to pay later when the agents diverge. Working in one shared space surfaces conflicts immediately so you can deal with them, which is easy when agents can communicate. Instead of isolating agents, Agent Mail coordinates them.
 
-**Advisory file reservations instead of hard locks.** There's a much better way to prevent conflicts than worktrees: agents call dibs temporarily on files while they're working on them, but it's not rigorously enforced, and reservations expire. Agents can observe if reserved files haven't been touched recently and reclaim them. This is critical because you want a system that's robust to agents suddenly dying or getting their memory wiped -- that happens all the time. Hard locks would deadlock.

You don't want ringleader agents whose death takes down the whole system. Agent Mail identities are memorable (e.g., `GreenCastle`), but ephemeral by design.

 ## Design Philosophy
 
-**Mail metaphor, not chat.** Agents send discrete messages with subjects, recipients, and thread IDs. This maps cleanly to how work coordination actually happens: structured communication with clear intent, not a firehose of chat messages. It would be like if your email system at work defaulted to reply-all every time -- that's what chat-based coordination does, and it burns context fast.

Imagine if your email system at work defaulted to reply-all every time; that's what chat-based coordination does, and it burns context fast.
 
-**Git as the source of truth.** Every message, agent profile, and reservation artifact lives as a file in a per-project Git repository. This makes the entire communication history human-auditable, diffable, and recoverable. SQLite is the fast index, not the authority.

-**Advisory, not mandatory.** File reservations are advisory leases, not hard locks. The pre-commit guard enforces them at commit time, but agents can always override if needed. +## Agent Configuration

+The `am` command auto-detects installed agents, but you can also configure them manually.

+Add to your project's `.mcp.json` or `~/.claude/settings.json`:
+

+{

+      "args": []
+    }
+  }

+```

+
+```json
+{
+  "mcpServers": {
+    "agent-mail": {
+      "type": "url",
+      "url": "http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/"
+    }
+  }

+```

+sequenceDiagram

+## Web UI
+
+The server includes a lightweight, server-rendered web UI for humans at `/mail/`. Agents should continue using MCP tools and resources; the web UI is for human review and oversight.
+
+### Routes
+
+| Route | What You See |
+|-------|-------------|
+| `/mail/` | Unified inbox across all projects, project list, related project suggestions |
+| `/mail/{project}` | Project overview with search, agent roster, quick links to reservations and attachments |
+| `/mail/{project}/inbox/{agent}` | Reverse-chronological inbox for one agent with pagination |
+| `/mail/{project}/message/{id}` | Full message detail with metadata, thread context, and attachments |
+| `/mail/{project}/search?q=...` | FTS5-powered search with field filters (`subject:foo`, `body:"multi word"`) |
+| `/mail/{project}/file_reservations` | Active and historical file reservations |
+| `/mail/{project}/attachments` | Messages with attachments |
+
+Auth: set `HTTP_BEARER_TOKEN` for production. For local dev, set `HTTP_ALLOW_LOCALHOST_UNAUTHENTICATED=true` to browse without headers.
+
+### Human Overseer
+
+Sometimes you need to redirect agents mid-session. The **Overseer** compose form at `/mail/{project}/overseer/compose` lets humans send high-priority messages directly to any combination of agents.
+
+Overseer messages:
+
+- Come from a special `HumanOverseer` agent (program: `WebUI`, model: `Human`)
+- Are always marked **high importance** so they stand out in agent inboxes
+- Bypass normal contact policies so you can always reach any agent
+- Include a preamble instructing agents to pause current work, handle the request, then resume
+
+Agents see overseer messages in their normal inbox via `fetch_inbox` or `resource://inbox/{name}`. They can reply in-thread like any other message. Everything is stored identically to agent-to-agent messages (Git + SQLite), fully auditable.

+## Mailbox Diagnostics (`am doctor`)
+
+Over time, mailbox state can drift: stale locks from crashed processes, orphaned records, FTS index desync, expired reservations piling up. The `doctor` command group detects and repairs these issues.
+
+```bash
+# Run diagnostics (fast, non-destructive)
+am doctor check
+am doctor check --verbose
+am doctor check --json          # Machine-readable output
+
+# Repair (creates backup first, prompts before data changes)
+am doctor repair --dry-run      # Preview what would change
+am doctor repair                # Apply safe fixes, prompt for data fixes
+am doctor repair --yes          # Auto-confirm everything (CI/automation)
+
+# Backup management
+am doctor backups               # List available backups
+am doctor restore /path/to/backup.sqlite3
+```
+
+What `check` inspects:
+
+| Check | Detects |
+|-------|---------|
+| Locks | Stale `.archive.lock` / `.commit.lock` from crashed processes |
+| Database | SQLite corruption via `PRAGMA integrity_check` |
+| Orphaned records | Message recipients without corresponding agents |
+| FTS index | Message count vs FTS index entry mismatch |
+| File reservations | Expired reservations pending cleanup |
+| WAL files | Orphan SQLite WAL/SHM files |
+
+The repair command applies safe fixes (stale locks, expired reservations) automatically and prompts before data-affecting changes (orphan cleanup, FTS rebuild). A backup is always created before any modifications.
